Reverse-Engineered Question:
A local government orally agreed with a towing company that the company would provide towing and storage services under a government towing program for five years, and the government would pay the company under the agreed schedule. Both sides understood from the start that the arrangement was meant to last for the full five-year program, not on an at-will basis. The towing company began performance, but before the first year ended, the program was shut down and the government stopped using the company. No signed writing was exchanged. Is the agreement enforceable? Analyze.

responses.responseText:
Probably not, at least not as an enforceable five-year contract.

The core issue is the Statute of Frauds. An oral agreement is unenforceable if, by its terms, it cannot be fully performed within one year from the time it is made. Here, both parties understood from the outset that the towing arrangement was for the full five-year government program and was not terminable at will. That means complete performance, as contemplated by the parties, could not occur within one year. So the agreement falls within the one-year provision of the Statute of Frauds and ordinarily requires a signed writing.

The fact that the program was shut down before the first year ended does not save the oral contract. The one-year rule is judged at the time of contracting: could the contract, as made, be fully performed within one year? A five-year service commitment cannot be. Early termination because the government later discontinued the program is not “full performance”; it is just premature cessation.

The towing company’s beginning performance also usually does not make the entire five-year oral contract enforceable. For contracts governed by the one-year provision, part performance generally does not take the contract out of the Statute of Frauds in the same way it can for land contracts. The company can usually recover for the value of services actually rendered, or for payments due for work already performed, but not expectation damages for the unperformed balance of the five-year term.

There is an additional and likely stronger problem: because one party is a local government, municipal contracting rules often require formal approval and a written contract executed in the prescribed manner. If those requirements apply, an oral agreement may be invalid even apart from the Statute of Frauds. Courts are often reluctant to bind public entities on informal promises, especially for multi-year obligations, and equitable doctrines like estoppel are applied narrowly against governments.

So the best analysis is:

1. The oral five-year agreement is within the Statute of Frauds because it could not be fully performed within one year.
2. No signed writing exists, so it is unenforceable as a contract for the remaining term.
3. Early shutdown of the program does not satisfy the one-year rule.
4. The towing company may still have a claim for compensation for services actually performed before termination, likely in contract for accrued payments or in quantum meruit/unjust enrichment.
5. If local-government procurement law required a written, formally approved contract, that is an independent reason the agreement is unenforceable.

Conclusion: the oral agreement is not enforceable as a binding five-year contract against the local government. The towing company’s likely remedy is limited to payment for services already provided, not damages for the lost remainder of the five-year term.

Probably not, at least not as an enforceable five-year contract.

The basic contract elements are present: the local government and the towing company orally agreed on services and compensation, and the company began performing. But the main problem is the Statute of Frauds.

A contract that, by its terms, cannot be performed within one year from the time it is made must generally be evidenced by a signed writing. This was a five-year service arrangement. The facts also make clear that both sides understood it was to last for the full five-year program and was not terminable at will. That matters, because if the arrangement had been at will, some courts would say it could end within a year and therefore might fall outside the one-year provision. Here, though, the parties intended a fixed five-year commitment, so full performance within one year was impossible.

The government’s shutdown of the program before the first year ended does not save the agreement. The one-year rule asks whether the contract, when made, was capable of full performance within a year. Early termination, breach, cancellation, impossibility, or frustration are not the same as performance. So the fact that the relationship actually ended within a year does not take the oral agreement out of the Statute of Frauds.

Part performance also usually does not make this kind of oral services contract enforceable for the full term. Unlike some land-sale cases, partial performance of a multi-year service contract generally does not overcome the one-year Statute of Frauds bar. So the towing company ordinarily cannot enforce the oral promise for the remaining years or recover expectation damages for the lost balance of the five-year term.

What the company may still recover is narrower. It can usually recover for services already rendered and accepted—either under the agreed rate for completed towing/storage work, or at least in restitution/quantum meruit for the value of the benefit conferred. But that is recovery for past performance, not enforcement of the full five-year bargain.

There is an additional obstacle because the counterparty is a local government. Public entities are often bound only if contracting formalities are followed—such as approval by the proper body, compliance with procurement rules, and a signed written contract. If those formalities were required and not satisfied, the agreement may be unenforceable on that separate ground as well. Courts are often especially reluctant to use estoppel to enforce an informal government promise that did not comply with statutory contracting requirements.

So the best answer is: the oral five-year agreement is generally not enforceable as a contract for the full five-year term, because it falls within the Statute of Frauds and lacks a signed writing; and government-contract formalities may independently defeat enforcement. The towing company’s likely remedy is limited to payment or restitution for services actually performed before the program was shut down.

Probably not, at least not as an enforceable five-year contract.

This is an oral services agreement for a fixed term of five years. Under the Statute of Frauds, a contract that by its terms cannot be fully performed within one year from the time it is made must generally be evidenced by a signed writing by the party to be charged. A five-year towing-and-storage arrangement plainly falls within that rule. The key point is that the contract, as agreed, required performance over five years; it was not terminable at will, and both sides understood it was supposed to last the full program term.

The fact that the government shut the program down before the first year ended does not take the contract outside the one-year provision. The legal question is whether the agreement could have been fully performed within one year according to its terms, not whether it might end early because of breach, termination, or outside events. Early shutdown is not “performance” of a five-year promise; it is simply nonperformance or cancellation.

So absent a signed writing, the towing company generally cannot enforce the oral agreement for the remaining four-plus years of expected payments or lost profits.

The company’s part performance also usually does not save this kind of oral contract. For service contracts, partial performance does not ordinarily remove the one-year Statute of Frauds bar. Full performance by one side sometimes can make an oral agreement enforceable, but that did not happen here; the company had only begun performance.

What the company may still recover is the value of what it actually provided before the shutdown. Courts often allow restitution or quantum meruit for services rendered and accepted, even when the larger oral contract is unenforceable under the Statute of Frauds. So the company may be able to collect for towing/storage services already performed under the agreed rate schedule or at least their reasonable value.

Because the counterparty is a local government, there is an additional obstacle. Municipal and local government contracts often must comply with statutory formalities, such as approval procedures and written execution requirements. If those formalities were not followed, the agreement may be not just unenforceable under the Statute of Frauds, but void as a government contract. Courts are typically especially reluctant to bind a public entity on an oral long-term agreement, and doctrines like estoppel are applied narrowly against government bodies.

Bottom line: the oral five-year agreement is likely unenforceable as a contract for the remaining term because it falls within the Statute of Frauds and lacks a signed writing, and government-contract formalities make enforcement even less likely. The towing company’s best claim is for payment for services already rendered, not for enforcement of the full five-year deal.

3. No signed writing exists, so the five-year contract is unenforceable.

# Enforceability of the Oral Five-Year Towing Services Agreement

This question presents a classic intersection of the Statute of Frauds, the one-year provision, and the doctrine of partial performance. The central issue is whether an oral agreement that, by its terms, cannot be performed within one year from the making of the contract is enforceable despite the absence of a signed writing.

## The Statute of Frauds – One-Year Provision

Under the traditional Statute of Frauds (adopted in virtually every jurisdiction either by statute or common law), a contract that by its terms is not to be performed within one year from the date of its making must be evidenced by a writing signed by the party to be charged. Because the oral agreement here expressly contemplated a five-year term—and both parties understood it was not at-will but was intended to run the full duration of the program—the agreement squarely falls within the one-year provision. It could not, by its terms, be fully performed within one year of its making.

Absent an applicable exception, the agreement is **unenforceable** under the Statute of Frauds. The government (the party to be charged) can raise the Statute as an affirmative defense.

## Could the Agreement Be Characterized as Performable Within One Year?

Some courts narrowly construe the one-year provision, holding that if there is any possibility—however remote—that performance could be completed within one year, the Statute does not apply. For example, contracts for a lifetime of services are often held outside the Statute because the promisor could die within a year, thereby completing performance.

Here, however, the parties expressly agreed to a **fixed five-year term**. There is no plausible reading under which full performance on both sides could occur within one year. The narrow-construction escape valve does not apply.

## Partial Performance as a Potential Exception

The towing company began performing under the agreement—it actually provided towing and storage services before the program was shut down. The question is whether partial performance removes the oral agreement from the Statute of Frauds.

**Under the majority common-law rule for service contracts**, partial performance does **not** take an oral agreement outside the one-year provision of the Statute of Frauds. This distinguishes the one-year provision from the real property provision (where part performance—such as taking possession, making improvements, or paying part of the purchase price—is a well-recognized exception). Courts reason that the one-year provision serves a distinct evidentiary function and that merely beginning to perform does not cure the risk of fraud or faulty memory over a multi-year term.

Therefore, under the majority rule, the towing company's partial performance does not make the five-year oral agreement enforceable.

## Restitution / Quasi-Contract as a Remedy

Even though the oral agreement itself is unenforceable, the towing company is not without remedy. Under well-established principles of unjust enrichment and quasi-contract, a party who has conferred a benefit on another under an unenforceable contract may recover the **reasonable value of services actually rendered**. The Statute of Frauds bars enforcement of the executory contract, but it does not permit the government to retain the benefit of services already performed without paying for them.

The towing company can therefore recover in **quantum meruit** for the towing and storage services it actually provided before the program was shut down. However, it cannot recover expectation damages (i.e., lost profits for the remaining years of the five-year term).

## Promissory Estoppel / Equitable Estoppel

Under **Restatement (Second) of Contracts § 139**, a promise that the promisor should reasonably expect to induce action or forbearance, and that does induce such action or forbearance, may be enforceable notwithstanding the Statute of Frauds if injustice can be avoided only by enforcement. Some courts have applied this doctrine to the one-year provision.

The towing company could argue that it reasonably relied on the government's oral promise of a five-year engagement—perhaps by purchasing equipment, hiring staff, or forgoing other business opportunities. If the company can demonstrate substantial, foreseeable, and detrimental reliance, a court exercising equity might enforce the promise to the extent necessary to prevent injustice. However, courts apply § 139 cautiously, and many jurisdictions are reluctant to use promissory estoppel to override the one-year provision, particularly in a commercial context between sophisticated parties.

## The Government as a Party – Additional Considerations

Because one party is a **local government**, additional concerns arise. Many jurisdictions require government contracts above a certain value or duration to be in writing and approved through formal procurement processes. Even apart from the general Statute of Frauds, a local government ordinance or state statute may independently require a written, signed contract for multi-year service agreements. If such a provision exists, it would provide an independent ground for unenforceability, and equitable doctrines like estoppel are often applied even more restrictively against government entities to protect the public fisc.

## Conclusion

The oral five-year agreement is **unenforceable** under the Statute of Frauds' one-year provision. The fixed five-year term cannot be performed within one year, and partial performance under the majority rule does not remove a services contract from the one-year provision. The towing company's most reliable remedy is **restitution in quantum meruit** for services actually rendered. A promissory estoppel argument under § 139 is available but faces significant doctrinal resistance, especially against a government entity. The absence of any signed writing is fatal to enforcement of the executory portion of the agreement.

# Enforceability of the Oral Five-Year Towing Services Agreement

This question requires analysis of the Statute of Frauds, the part-performance doctrine, and potentially promissory estoppel to determine whether the oral agreement is enforceable.

## The Statute of Frauds Problem

Under the traditional Statute of Frauds, an agreement that by its terms cannot be performed within one year from the date of its making must be evidenced by a writing signed by the party to be charged. Here, the oral agreement contemplated a five-year term of performance. Because the agreement could not, by its terms, be fully performed within one year, it falls squarely within the one-year provision of the Statute of Frauds. Without a signed writing, the agreement is presumptively unenforceable under this rule.

It is important to note the narrow construction courts sometimes give the one-year provision: if there is *any* possibility that the contract could be fully performed within one year (e.g., if performance could be accelerated, or if a contingency such as death could discharge obligations), some courts will hold the Statute inapplicable. However, a fixed five-year term with no such contingency does not admit of full performance within one year. The one-year rule therefore applies.

## Part Performance as an Exception

The part-performance doctrine is most robustly recognized in the context of contracts for the sale of land (another Statute of Frauds category), where acts such as taking possession, making improvements, and paying consideration can take the contract out of the Statute. Courts are divided on whether part performance can remove an oral contract from the one-year provision of the Statute of Frauds.

Here, the towing company did begin performing—providing towing and storage services—before the government terminated the program. In jurisdictions that recognize part performance as an exception to the one-year rule, the company's commencement and continuation of performance, combined with the government's acceptance of those services and (presumably) partial payment, could supply the evidentiary and reliance basis that the Statute is designed to ensure. The rationale is that the party's conduct corroborates the existence of the agreement, thereby satisfying the Statute's evidentiary purpose.

However, many jurisdictions hold that part performance does *not* take an oral agreement out of the one-year provision (as distinguished from the land-sale provision). Under the Restatement (Second) of Contracts §139, the relevant doctrine in such cases is promissory estoppel rather than part performance per se.

## Promissory Estoppel / Reliance-Based Enforcement

Absent a signed writing, the contract is presumptively unenforceable under this provision.

It is worth noting the narrow construction many courts give this provision: the question is whether the contract is **capable** of being performed within one year, not whether it is **likely** to be. However, a fixed five-year term cannot, by its own terms, be completed within one year. Unlike an agreement to perform services "for life" (which could theoretically end within a year if the person dies), a five-year fixed term is definitionally impossible to complete in one year. The one-year provision therefore applies.

## Part Performance as an Exception

The towing company did begin performing under the agreement. The part-performance exception, however, is applied differently depending on context. In the sale-of-goods context under UCC § 2-201, part performance can render a contract enforceable to the extent of goods delivered and accepted. In the real property context, part performance (possession, improvements, payment) is a well-recognized exception.

For contracts falling under the one-year provision, however, the part-performance doctrine is more limited. The **Restatement (Second) of Contracts § 129** addresses part performance primarily in the land-contract setting. Under **§ 139**, a promise that the promisor should reasonably expect to induce reliance, and that does induce reliance, may be enforceable notwithstanding the Statute of Frauds if injustice can be avoided only by enforcement. Many jurisdictions, though, are reluctant to apply a broad part-performance exception to the one-year rule for service contracts. The traditional rule is that **mere partial performance of a service contract does not take it out of the one-year provision** of the Statute of Frauds. The rationale is that partial performance of a multi-year service contract is not sufficiently "unequivocally referable" to the alleged oral agreement to overcome the evidentiary concerns the Statute addresses.

Some modern courts, however, have relaxed this rule and hold that substantial part performance, combined with other corroborating evidence, may estop a party from raising the Statute of Frauds as a defense. This is particularly true where one party has changed position significantly in reliance on the agreement.

## Promissory Estoppel / Reliance-Based Enforcement

Under **Restatement (Second) § 139**, a court may enforce an oral promise notwithstanding the Statute of Frauds where:

1. The promisor should reasonably have expected the promise to induce action or forbearance;
2. The promise did induce such action or forbearance; and
3. Injustice can be avoided only by enforcing the promise.

Here, the local government knew the towing company would invest resources—equipment, personnel, scheduling, and opportunity costs—in reliance on a five-year arrangement. The company did in fact begin performing. If the company turned away other business, purchased or allocated vehicles, or otherwise detrimentally relied on the five-year term, a court could find the elements of § 139 satisfied. The remedy under § 139 may be limited, however; courts sometimes enforce the promise only to the extent necessary to prevent injustice, which may mean reliance damages rather than full expectation damages.

## Quasi-Contract / Unjust Enrichment

Even if the oral agreement is unenforceable, the towing company can recover in **quantum meruit** for the reasonable value of services actually rendered. The government received towing and storage services, and allowing it to retain the benefit of those services without paying would constitute unjust enrichment. This theory does not enforce the contract itself but provides restitutionary recovery for benefits conferred.

## The Government-Contract Wrinkle

An additional concern arises because one party is a **local government**. Many jurisdictions require government contracts above a certain value or duration to be in writing, approved by the appropriate governing body, and executed in compliance with procurement statutes or ordinances. If applicable local law requires competitive bidding, council approval, or a signed written contract for multi-year service agreements, the oral agreement may be void—not merely voidable—as ultra vires or in violation of public contracting requirements. This would be an independent ground for unenforceability beyond the general Statute of Frauds. Even so, quasi-contractual recovery for services actually rendered to and accepted by the government is generally still available, as courts are reluctant to let a government entity benefit from services without compensation.

## Conclusion

The oral five-year agreement is likely **unenforceable as to its full five-year term** under the Statute of Frauds' one-year provision. Part performance alone is unlikely to remove the contract from the Statute in most jurisdictions when the one-year rule is at issue. However, the towing company has two avenues of potential recovery: **(1)** promissory estoppel under Restatement § 139, if the company can demonstrate detrimental reliance and injustice, which may yield reliance or limited expectation damages; and **(2)** quantum meruit for the reasonable value of services already performed. If the jurisdiction applies additional government-contracting formalities, those requirements independently bar enforcement of the agreement as a contract, but restitutionary recovery remains available for services rendered and accepted.

# Enforceability of the Oral Five-Year Towing Services Agreement

This question requires analysis of the Statute of Frauds, the part-performance doctrine, and potentially promissory estoppel or quasi-contractual recovery.

## The Statute of Frauds Problem

Under the traditional Statute of Frauds, an agreement that by its terms cannot be performed within one year from the date of its making must be evidenced by a writing signed by the party to be charged. Here, the oral agreement contemplated a five-year term of performance. Because the contract could not, by its terms, be fully performed within one year, it falls squarely within the one-year provision of the Statute of Frauds. Without a signed writing, the agreement is presumptively unenforceable as a matter of law.

It is worth noting the narrow construction many courts give this provision: the question is whether performance is *possible* within one year, not whether it is *likely*. However, a fixed five-year term cannot be completed within one year. Unlike an agreement for lifetime services (which could theoretically end within a year due to death), a definite five-year term is the classic case the one-year rule was designed to capture. The agreement is therefore within the Statute.

## Part Performance as an Exception

The part-performance doctrine is most robustly applied in the context of real property contracts, where courts will enforce an oral agreement if the party seeking enforcement has taken actions—such as making improvements, paying consideration, or taking possession—that are unequivocally referable to the contract and would result in fraud or injustice if the Statute were used as a defense.

Outside the land context, courts are divided on whether part performance removes an oral contract from the one-year provision. The **majority rule** is that partial performance of a contract that falls within the one-year provision does **not** take the agreement outside the Statute of Frauds. The reasoning is that the one-year provision, unlike the land or goods provisions, is not as readily amenable to the part-performance exception because the acts of performance (here, towing cars) are not "unequivocally referable" to a specific five-year oral agreement as opposed to some shorter or at-will arrangement.

A **minority of jurisdictions**, and the **Restatement (Second) of Contracts § 139**, take a more flexible approach, recognizing that where one party has substantially performed and the other party has accepted the benefits of that performance, enforcing the Statute rigidly would work an injustice. Under this view, the towing company's commencement and continuation of performance, combined with the government's acceptance of those services, could supply the evidentiary and cautionary functions the Statute is meant to serve.

Here, the towing company did begin performance and provided services. The government accepted and presumably paid for those services during the period of performance. However, under the majority approach, this partial performance alone would not render the five-year oral agreement enforceable for its remaining term.

## Promissory Estoppel / Reliance

Under **Restatement (Second) § 139**, a promise that the promisor should reasonably expect to induce action or forbearance and that does induce such action or forbearance is enforceable notwithstanding the Statute of Frauds if injustice can be avoided only by enforcement. Courts consider: (1) the availability and adequacy of other remedies, (2) the definite and substantial character of the reliance, (3) the degree to which the reliance corroborates the existence of the oral agreement, and (4) the reasonableness of the reliance.

The towing company likely invested in equipment, staffing, and infrastructure to service a five-year government contract. If the company can demonstrate definite, substantial, and reasonable reliance—and that the government should have foreseen such reliance—a court following § 139 could enforce the promise to the extent necessary to prevent injustice. This might mean enforcing the full five-year term or, more conservatively, awarding reliance damages.

However, many courts are reluctant to apply promissory estoppel against a government entity, and some jurisdictions have statutes or doctrines requiring government contracts above a certain duration or value to be in writing, separate from the general Statute of Frauds.

## Quasi-Contract / Restitution

Even if the oral agreement is unenforceable, the towing company would have a strong claim in **quantum meruit** or **unjust enrichment** for the reasonable value of services already rendered. The government cannot accept the benefit of the company's towing and storage services and then invoke the Statute to avoid paying for them. This recovery, however, would be limited to the value of services actually performed—not expectation damages for the remaining years of the contract.

## Conclusion

Under the prevailing majority rule, the oral five-year agreement is likely **unenforceable** for its full term because it falls within the one-year provision of the Statute of Frauds and partial performance generally does not cure that defect for service contracts. The towing company can recover in restitution for services already rendered. In a jurisdiction following the Restatement (Second) § 139 approach, the company may have a stronger argument for enforcement based on reasonable and substantial reliance, but even then, full enforcement of the remaining term is uncertain—courts may limit relief to reliance damages rather than the expectation interest in the full five-year arrangement.

However, a fixed five-year term with no such contingency does not admit of full performance within one year. The one-year rule therefore applies.

## Part Performance as an Exception

The part-performance doctrine is most robustly recognized in the context of contracts for the sale of land (another Statute of Frauds category), where acts such as taking possession, making improvements, and paying consideration can take the contract out of the Statute. Courts are divided on whether part performance can remove an oral contract from the one-year provision of the Statute of Frauds.

Here, the towing company did begin performing—providing towing and storage services—before the government terminated the program. In jurisdictions that recognize part performance as an exception to the one-year rule, the company's commencement and continuation of performance, combined with the government's acceptance of those services and (presumably) partial payment, could supply the evidentiary and reliance basis that the Statute is designed to ensure. The rationale is that the party's conduct corroborates the existence of the agreement, thereby satisfying the Statute's evidentiary purpose.

However, many jurisdictions hold that part performance does *not* take an oral agreement out of the one-year provision (as distinguished from the land-sale provision). Under the Restatement (Second) of Contracts §139, the relevant doctrine in such cases is promissory estoppel rather than part performance per se.

## Promissory Estoppel / Reliance-Based Enforcement

Under Restatement (Second) §139, a promise that the promisor should reasonably expect to induce action or forbearance, and that does induce such action or forbearance, is enforceable notwithstanding the Statute of Frauds if injustice can be avoided only by enforcement. Relevant factors include:

1. **Reasonableness and foreseeability of reliance.** The government knew the company would invest in equipment, personnel, and operational capacity to serve a five-year program. This reliance was foreseeable and reasonable.

2. **The extent and nature of the action taken.** The company actually began performing towing and storage services, likely incurring significant costs (equipment, facility preparation, staffing, foregoing other business opportunities).

3. **Availability of other remedies.** If the company cannot recover under the contract, it may have a quasi-contractual claim for services already rendered (quantum meruit), but this would not compensate for expectation damages—lost profits over the remaining term.

4. **The degree to which the evidentiary function of the Statute is satisfied.** Both parties apparently agree an arrangement existed; the dispute is not about whether a deal was made but about its enforceability in the absence of a writing. The company's performance and the government's acceptance of services supply strong corroboration.

Under §139, a court could enforce the promise to the extent necessary to prevent injustice, which might mean full enforcement of the five-year term or, more conservatively, enforcement limited to reliance damages.

## Quantum Meruit as a Fallback

Even if the oral agreement is held unenforceable, the towing company would have a strong claim in quasi-contract (unjust enrichment / quantum meruit) for the reasonable value of services actually rendered. The government received the benefit of towing and storage services and would be unjustly enriched if it paid nothing. This recovery, however, is measured by the value of services conferred, not by the contract price or expectation interest.

## Additional Considerations: Government Contracts

Many jurisdictions impose additional formality requirements on government contracts—statutory provisions requiring that contracts with governmental entities be in writing, approved by the appropriate authority, and sometimes subject to competitive bidding. If such a statute applies, it may independently bar enforcement regardless of the common-law Statute of Frauds analysis. Courts are often stricter about enforcing formality requirements for government contracts because of public accountability concerns, and equitable doctrines like estoppel are sometimes held inapplicable against the government (under the doctrine that estoppel generally does not run against the sovereign).

## Conclusion

The oral agreement is likely **unenforceable as a five-year executory contract** under the Statute of Frauds' one-year provision, absent a writing. However, the towing company has strong arguments for partial or full enforcement under **promissory estoppel (Restatement §139)** given its foreseeable, reasonable, and substantial reliance. At minimum, the company can recover in **quantum meruit** for the reasonable value of services already performed. The strongest barrier to enforcement may be **government contract formality requirements**, which could independently defeat the claim and limit equitable remedies. The most likely outcome is that the company recovers for services rendered but may not recover full expectation damages for the remaining term of the five-year arrangement.
